source: Future Policy in Malaya - Draft proclamations__CO273:67:59
filename: Future Policy in Malaya - Draft proclamations__CO273:67:59.txt
summary:
- **Core purpose/topic:** Internal British government/War Office–Colonial Office coordination on **“Future Policy in Malaya”** through drafting, legal vetting, and printing of **proclamations and rules** to govern Malaya immediately after liberation from Japanese occupation under **British Military Administration (BMA)**.  
- **Geography & period:** **Malaya** (Singapore, Penang, Malacca; Malay States including Perak, Selangor, Negri Sembilan, Pahang, Johore, Kedah, Kelantan, Trengganu, Perlis) during **1945** (with follow-up legal-planning telegrams in **Jan 1946**).  
- **Major decision/controversy (“martial law” vs “military administration”):** Extensive discussion in mid-1945 over whether Proclamation No. 1 (and related Military Courts proclamation) should explicitly use **“martial law”**; War Office decided to **omit “martial law”** and use **“military administration”** (Burma precedent), despite SACSEA raising legal/constitutional concerns. Attorney General (David Maxwell Fyfe) advised there was **no insuperable legal objection** to omission; later Colonial Office accepted avoiding “martial law” for consistency with Burma.  
- **Key proclamations and policy areas covered:** Drafts include establishing BMA and suspending/reopening courts; **Military Courts** provisions; **Delegation of Powers** (dividing Malaya into Malay Peninsula and Singapore divisions); **Currency and Exchange** controls (incl. treatment of $1,000 and $10,000 notes); **Custodian of Property**; **Food Control and rationing** (modelled on Palestine ordinance); **Road Transport** regulation; plus references to **Moratorium**, **Requisitioning**, **Opium and Chandu**, and other emergency regulations/manuals.  
- **Venereal Disease (V.D.) proclamation dispute:** A separate draft sought to make **V.D. a “notifiable/infectious disease”**; legal/medical advisers (notably Y.H. Hastings) objected that it was **too wide**, risked **blackmail**, and violated confidentiality principles. Despite misgivings, urgency and reports of severe V.D. in Singapore led to allowing printing/proceeding, while recommending limiting powers mainly to **entry and removal-to-hospital powers** (cited as Sections **8 and 10** of the F.M.S. Prevention of Disease Enactment).  
- **Key actors/organizations:** Colonial Office officials (e.g., **H.T. Bourdillon**, **G.E.J. Gent**, **W.B.L. Monson**, **W.S. Morgan**), War Office/Civil Affairs staff (**Lt.-Col. K.K. O’Connor**, **J.B. Spencer Wilkinson**, **Lt.-Col. Logan**, **Col. Drew**), legal advisers including the **Attorney General (David Maxwell Fyfe)**, and command authorities **SACSEA** (Admiral **Lord Louis Mountbatten**), **GOC Malaya Lt-Gen M.C. Dempsey**, and **Chief Civil Affairs Officer Malaya Maj-Gen H. Ralph Hone**.  
- **Notable dates/numbers/logistics:** Drafting and approvals run **Jan–Oct 1945**; key instruments dated **1 Oct 1945** (Delegation of Powers Proclamation and warrants). Printing/air-despatch issues recur, with large print runs mentioned (e.g., **7,500** copies of several proclamations; **9,000** copies for Malay Peninsula courts/interpretation; **1,500** for Singapore; weights like **500 lbs** and **138 lbs** for air freight). **Jan 20–21, 1946** telegrams request BMA to identify which military proclamation provisions should remain after **resumption of civil government**.
